results.title: Physics-informed vs world models: a clash of AI/ML approaches | Raghvendra Cowlagi posted on the topic | LinkedIn
results.content: An outsider's perspective on some of the new developments in AI/ML. On the one hand, physics-informed neural networks have come up in the last few years. These are ML models that learn to solve known equations of physics that have proven difficult to solve otherwise. In that sense, these models make predictions by "understanding" physics. Or any other equations, like control laws. On the other hand, we are starting to see new "world models" like Genie 3 and V-JEPA 2, which produce physically realistic videos by observation of a large corpus of real-world (?) data, but without understanding any physics at all. So, these models seem be able to predict/generate Newtonian motion without ever knowing F = ma. These opposite approaches are bound to collide soon. There is already a wealth of [...] Sometimes I have this thought when I hear engineers talking about simulation of the physical world that is kind of nutty. They criticize AI models because they are not accurate or physical, but put their faith completely in traditional solvers because they are, right? (I mean finite elements or finite volumes or FDTD, these are the real thing!) I was trained to see these methods as approximations of differential equations, generally very prone to misbehave, stable only under a very narrow set of assumptions and conditions, and \\certainly not the real truth\\. The PDEs themselves are already approximations of reality, so traditional methods are approximations on top of approximations of the real physics on a very narrow set of conditions... Physics AI models are, in my head, \\just [...] are bound to collide soon. There is already a wealth of papers on physics-informed generative models, including our latest work on generative models for optimally controlled systems:  Engineering reality is indeed somewhere in the middle. It doesn't seem right to throw out centuries worth of our understanding of the world, neatly condensed into a few equations. These equations have simplifications and unknowns. Real-world observations can help fill in the blanks. #WorldModels

results.title: RoboScape: Physics-informed Embodied World Model | OpenReview
results.content:
Abstract: World models have become indispensable tools for embodied intelligence, serving as powerful simulators capable of generating realistic robotic videos while addressing critical data scarcity challenges. However, current embodied world models exhibit limited physical awareness, particularly in modeling 3D geometry and motion dynamics, resulting in unrealistic video generation for contact-rich robotic scenarios. In this paper, we present RoboScape, a unified physics-informed world model that jointly learns RGB video generation and physics knowledge within an integrated framework. We introduce two key physics-informed joint training tasks: temporal depth prediction that enhances 3D geometric consistency in video rendering, and keypoint dynamics learning that implicitly encodes [...] and keypoint dynamics learning that implicitly encodes physical properties (e.g., object shape and material characteristics) while improving complex motion modeling. Extensive experiments demonstrate that RoboScape generates videos with superior visual fidelity and physical plausibility across diverse robotic scenarios. We further validate its practical utility through downstream applications including robotic policy training with generated data and policy evaluation. Our work provides new insights for building efficient physics-informed world models to advance embodied intelligence research. Our code and demos are available at: [...] Supplementary Material:  zip

Primary Area: Deep learning (e.g., architectures, generative models, optimization for deep networks, foundation models, LLMs)

Submission Number: 20698

results.title: The Imperative of Physical Grounding in World Models - arXiv
results.content: Crucially, the constraints governing medical environments differ fundamentally from those in classical physical control systems. Although robotic manipulation benefits from explicit rigid-body dynamics and kinematic laws, internal medicine is shaped instead by pathophysiological processes, homeostatic regulation, and biochemical interactions. As such, the appropriate objective is not a narrowly construed “physics-informed” model, but rather a domain-constrained, biologically grounded world model. Here, the governing “laws” are clinical in nature: physiological viability ranges (e.g., blood pressure or oxygen saturation bounds), pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic rules, disease progression priors, and known causal asymmetries between intervention and outcome. These constraints serve an [...] A world model is an AI system that simulates how an environment evolves under actions, enabling planning through imagined futures rather than reactive perception. Current world models, however, suffer from visual conflation: the mistaken assumption that high-fidelity video generation implies an understanding of physical and causal dynamics. We show that while modern models excel at predicting pixels, they frequently violate invariant constraints, fail under intervention, and break down in safety-critical decision-making. This survey argues that visual realism is an unreliable proxy for world understanding. Instead, effective world models must encode causal structure, respect domain-specific constraints, and remain stable over long horizons. We propose a reframing of world models as [...] ## 4 Self-Evolution with Physical Anchors: Physics-Informed World Models

results.title: PIN-WM
results.content: We propose PIN-WM, a Physics-INformed World Model that enables efficient end-to-end identification of a 3D rigid body dynamical system from visual observations. Adopting differentiable physics simulation, PIN-WM can be learned with only few-shot and task-agnostic physical interaction trajectories. Further, PIN-WM is learned with observational loss induced by Gaussian Splatting without needing state estimation. To bridge Sim2Real gaps, we turn the learned PIN-WM into a group of Digital Cousins via physics-aware perturbations which perturb physics and rendering parameters to generate diverse and meaningful variations of the PIN-WM.
